articles.title: The ISO 20022 Crypto Narrative Gets the Blockchain Story Backwards
articles.description: The hype says SWIFT's ISO 20022 migration will send "ISO 20022 coins" to the moon. It's backwards. The November 2026 deadline barely touches on-chain transfers; it bites at the fiat edges, where exchanges and issuers meet the banks. The quiet effect: compliant, freezable coins like USDC and USDT get a regulatory moat, Chainlink lets SWIFT absorb the blockchain rails rather than be disrupted, and Tether's USDT0 bets on its own cross-border rail. The real Trojan horse isn't a coin — it's the freeze switch and identity layer becoming native infrastructure.

articles.description: Milano Centrale is the main train station in northern Italy and the second-largest station in Italy behind Roma Termini . The building was officially opened on July 1, 1931, replacing the city's first central station, which opened in 1864. The construction was intended to showcase the power of then-Prime Minister Mussolini's fascist regime, with a notorious scale, massive arches, and an imposing facade. Following a private competition promoted by Grandi Stazioni Retail , Park, an Italian interdisciplinary collective , was selected to redesign the station's ground floor and mezzanine levels, transforming the historic city landmark into a contemporary urban platform.

articles.title: Meta hits pause on tracking employee keystrokes to train AI after internal leak
articles.description: Just two months after Meta announced plans to track employee keystrokes and mouse movements to train its AI models, the company said it would pause the program after an alleged internal leak. According to a screenshot obtained by Business Insider, employees’ private conversations, performance data and transcriptions were accessible across the company. On a scale of 0 to 5—0 being the most severe—the incident was classified as a SEV (severity) 2. Meta confirmed the incident to Business Insider. According to documents reviewed by Wired , a security notice said that “employee data across 45,000 hive tables”—a data storage format—were exposed, which included employee activity, including “full prompts and transcriptions, private conversations, people and performance data.” In response to Fast Company’ s request for comment, a Meta spokesperson said: “We have carefully designed this program with privacy safeguards and while we have no indication at this time that any data was improperly accessed by Meta employees, we’re pausing it while we investigate.” Meta employees had strong reactions to the internal AI training model, called the Model Capability Initiative, or MCI. Staffers could not opt out of being tracked by the software on company laptops, which gave rise to privacy concerns. Following this tension—and 10% of Meta’s workforce being laid off —the company’s CTO Andrew Bosworth said that company morale was “near the worst it’s ever been.” In May, Meta told Fast Company that its “[AI] models need real examples of how people actually use [computers]—things like mouse movements, clicking buttons, and navigating dropdown menus.” A spokesperson for the company also added that there were “safeguards in place to protect sensitive content.” Still, employees created an online petition protesting against the use of the software. They posted flyers across Meta offices calling on their colleagues to sign it. “When employees asked what privacy reviews were conducted, including any ‘people data reviews’ (which are required for processing employee data), no completed privacy reviews were provided,” the petition , which had 1,600 signatures as of June 3, read. “The outlined privacy mitigations were vague, and leadership’s confidence in them appeared limited—evidenced by the selective opt-out afforded to executives.”

articles.title: Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2 review: Reliable performance, smarter navigation
articles.description: At a glance Expert's Rating Pros Excellent accessibility Stable app Fast Well-built and practical design Cons Obstacle detection isn’t the best in its class Camera fault during testing Our Verdict The Sunseeker X7 performs excellently and has run reliably for nearly four weeks on a difficult plot, aside from a minor camera error. It’s a strong AWD option. Price at time of review This value will show the geolocated pricing text for product undefined Best Price Today Sunseeker’s Elite X series has long been among the most capable options in the premium class, and the new Elite X7 Gen 2 builds on the same core formula. The most obvious new feature is that the mower now has a built-in Net-RTK antenna. Its predecessor required an external antenna solution, whilst the new model can use the network to receive its correction data directly within the machine. For those who wish, there is still the option to add an external antenna. Inside, Sunseeker has updated the hardware with a more powerful system-on-chip designed to deliver faster calculations for navigation and object detection. Mikael Lindkvist Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2: A strange start The installation is relatively straightforward. The app guides you step by step through the process and mapping is quick. It’s easy to create zones, add restricted areas, and define routes between different parts of the property. At the same time, the start-up wasn’t entirely problem-free. During installation, I received repeated warnings about a poor RTK signal, even though the mower was positioned with a clear view of the sky and had good coverage. I never managed to find a clear explanation for why the warnings appeared. This created a certain amount of uncertainty while setting up the machine. After all, positioning is the very basis of how this type of robotic lawnmower works. The strange thing is that the problems essentially ceased once the installation was complete. For larger and more complex gardens, it remains one of the strongest contenders on the market. During a month of daily use, the Elite X7 Gen 2 has performed almost flawlessly. The mower has navigated with high precision, stayed within its zones, and delivered an even cut without requiring any significant supervision. The long-term performance is what impresses me the most. Many robotic lawnmowers work excellently during the first few days. Fewer manage to keep doing so week after week without problems. In my case, the Sunseeker pretty much run itself. Mikael Lindkvist Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2: Two zones with a long travel distance My test environment isn’t entirely straightforward. I use two separate lawns connected by a long passage across gravel. This type of layout usually poses a good challenge for the navigation system. The mower consistently finds its way between the zones and follows its route without hesitation or taking a wrong turn. The ability to configure each zone individually is also one of the model’s great strengths. Cutting height, operating times, and other settings can be adjusted separately for each area, and everything is done directly in the app in a logical, easy-to-understand way. Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2: Three-wheel drive remains a major asset The Sunseeker uses AWD (i.e. three-wheel drive), which provides a level of confidence on uneven surfaces and slopes that many competitors lack. The mower moves with such confidence that I rarely worried about it getting stuck. And it does so without damaging the lawn. I didn’t see any wheel tracks in front of the docking station. The mowing results are good, with excellent and consistent precision. The twin cutting discs provide a wide cutting width, and the robot is relatively fast, so it doesn’t have to work a full day to mow a large plot. The build quality remains impressive, too. The Elite X7 Gen 2 feels robust and well-designed. This is a machine that gives a significantly more premium impression than many competitors in the same category. Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2: Improved obstacle detection The updated chip is noticeable in obstacle handling. The mower is better at identifying and avoiding objects than its predecessor. It also feels more confident in its behavior and makes fewer hesitant maneuvers when it encounters obstacles in the working area. That said, I wouldn’t call it class-leading. There are still competitors that are more accurate and consistent when it comes to advanced object detection. For most users, the system will be perfectly adequate, but there is still room for improvement here. Mikael Lindkvist Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2: The app is one of the best The app remains one of the model’s strongest points. The interface is easy to understand, the functions are logically organized, and it’s quick to make changes. Small details also contribute to the overall impression. For example, if it’s raining where the mower is, the app will show a rainy sky in the background. Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2: A few minor drawbacks Although it has been very straightforward to use in practice, I’ve come across a few minor annoyances. Apart from the strange RTK warnings during installation, the app has, on a few occasions, reported camera errors. The error disappeared after restarting the mower, but I haven’t been able to determine the cause. Mikael Lindkvist The Sunseeker Elite X7 Gen 2 is, on the outside, identical to its predecessor. This is a well-thought-out upgrade featuring built-in Net-RTK, updated hardware, and improved obstacle detection. The installation raised a few questions, but once the mower was up and running, it has performed without interruption. For larger and more complex gardens, it remains one of the strongest contenders on the market. Specifications Manufacturer : Sunseeker Model : Elite X7 Gen 2 Tested : June 2026 Drive : AWD Mowing area : Up to 3,000 square meters Cutting height : 20–100 mm Cutting width : 24 cm Obstacle detection : AI Vision with camera and object detection Theft protection : PIN code, app tracking and geofencing Connectivity : Bluetooth, Wi-Fi and 4G Rain protection : Yes, rain sensor Maximum gradient : 70% Protection rating: IPX6 Navigation : Built-in NET-RTK, GPS and vision-based positioning, support for external RTK antenna Price : $2,499 USD Rating: 4 out of 5
